data.title: As a practitioner, debug-everything skips diagnostic/triage and fixes known bugs directly when open bug tickets exist
data.description:
## Description

**What**: Restructure debug-everything to detect open bug tickets at startup and enter bug-fix mode — skipping diagnostic scan and triage sub-agent dispatch, instead invoking fix-bug sequentially at the orchestrator level for each open bug
**Why**: When bugs already exist as tickets, diagnostic and triage phases are redundant — they re-discover what is already known, wasting wall-clock time and tokens
**Scope**:
- IN: New bug-fix mode entry path in debug-everything; sequential fix-bug invocation at orchestrator level; existing bug ticket detection logic
- OUT: Validation mode loop-back (Story 3); interactivity question (Story 4); modifying fix-bug's internal workflow

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, debug-everything invoked with open bug tickets dispatches no diagnostic or triage sub-agents
  ← Satisfies: "When debug-everything is invoked with open bug tickets, it skips diagnostic/triage phases"
- When this story is complete, fix-bug runs at the orchestrator level with full Agent tool access and its investigation sub-agents complete without nesting errors
  ← Satisfies: "fix-bug runs at the orchestrator level (not as a sub-agent) with full Agent tool access"
- When this story is complete, unit tests written and passing for all new or modified logic
  ← TDD Done-of-Done

## Considerations

- [Reliability] If fix-bug fails mid-investigation, debug-everything must handle cleanup without sub-agent isolation — errors should be caught and the next bug attempted
- [Testing] Integration test verifying fix-bug dispatches investigation sub-agents successfully from debug-everything orchestrator context
- [Maintainability] Both paths (bugs-exist vs no-bugs) must be maintained — ensure existing diagnostic flow is preserved for the no-bugs case
